title.emphasis: Part III: The Corporate Sphere: Consent as Commodity and Legal Fiction
Chapter 7: The Data Economy: Surveillance Capitalism, Data Brokerage, and the Façade of Consent
The Business Model of Data Brokerage

section.para: In the modern digital economy, personal information has become a highly valuable commodity, giving rise to the sprawling and largely unregulated industry of data brokerage. Data brokers are companies whose business model is predicated on the collection, aggregation, analysis, and sale of personal data about millions of individuals, typically without their direct knowledge or meaningful consent.92 These companies operate in the shadows of the internet, compiling extensive dossiers on consumers from a vast array of sources, including public records (voter registration, property records), commercial sources (credit card purchases, loyalty card programs), and online activities (browsing history, social media usage, app interactions).95 The collected data points—ranging from basic demographics to sensitive information like health concerns, political affiliations, and real-time location—are then used to create detailed consumer profiles and segments (e.g., "new mothers," "fitness enthusiasts") that are sold to other businesses for purposes like targeted advertising, risk mitigation, and fraud detection.92

title.emphasis: Credit Reporting Agencies and the FCRA

section.para: A prominent and federally sanctioned form of data brokerage is the credit reporting industry, dominated by three major agencies: Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion.98 These consumer reporting agencies (CRAs) non-consensually collect and compile the financial histories of hundreds of millions of Americans.98 This practice is governed by the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), a federal law enacted in 1970.98 The FCRA does not prohibit the collection of this data without consent. Instead, it legitimizes the industry by creating a regulatory framework that focuses on ensuring the accuracy of information and establishing consumer rights.98 Under the FCRA, consumers have the right to access their own credit reports, dispute inaccuracies, and be informed when a report has been used to take adverse action against them (e.g., denial of a loan).102 The Act also defines the "permissible purposes" for which a credit report can be used, such as for decisions regarding credit, insurance, employment, and housing.101 In essence, the FCRA codifies a system where consent is irrelevant to the collection of one's most sensitive financial data; the law's focus is on managing the use of that data after the fact.

In the broader digital ecosystem, the primary mechanism for obtaining permission for data collection is the "clickwrap" agreement—the ubiquitous "I have read and agree to the Terms of Service" checkbox that users must click to access a website or service. While this is legally treated as a form of express consent, it functions as a legal fiction. These agreements are often lengthy, filled with complex legal jargon, and presented on a take-it-or-leave-it basis, affording the user no real choice or bargaining power.93 By clicking "agree," individuals are often unknowingly consenting to sweeping data collection, use, and sharing practices that they have not read, let alone understood. This façade of consent provides legal cover for the vast, non-consensual data harvesting that fuels the business models of many technology companies and, in turn, supplies the data broker industry.

The U.S. approach to data privacy, which is fragmented and generally follows an "opt-out" model (where data collection is permissible by default unless a user actively objects), stands in stark contrast to the comprehensive framework established by the European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).108 The GDPR is built on an "opt-in" philosophy, making the processing of personal data illegal unless there is a specific, lawful basis for it.112 One of the primary lawful bases is consent, which the GDPR defines stringently as "freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous".112 This requires a clear, affirmative act from the user; pre-ticked boxes or implied consent are not valid.112 Furthermore, consent must be as easy to withdraw as it is to give.112 This fundamental difference in legal philosophy highlights the U.S. system's prioritization of commercial interests in data collection over the individual's fundamental right to privacy and control over their personal information, a right that is explicitly protected in the EU.111

title.emphasis: Chapter 8: The Public Square and the Private Lens: Corporate Surveillance in Physical Spaces

section.para: The proliferation of closed-circuit television (CCTV) and other video surveillance technologies has transformed public and semi-public spaces into areas of constant monitoring. The legal right of private entities—such as shopping malls, retail stores, and employers—to conduct video surveillance on their own property is well-established.114 The legal justification for this practice rests on two main pillars: the rights of the property owner to secure their premises and the legal doctrine that individuals do not have a "reasonable expectation of privacy" in places that are open to the public or in common areas of a workplace.114

In these contexts, consent from the individuals being filmed is not required. While businesses often post signs indicating that surveillance is in progress, this is frequently a matter of policy or a measure to deter misconduct rather than a strict legal mandate in all jurisdictions. Such signage also serves a legal function by further diminishing any claim to a reasonable expectation of privacy; by entering a space where surveillance is announced, an individual is often deemed to have forfeited any privacy rights in that location.114 However, this right to surveil is not unlimited. The law still recognizes a reasonable expectation of privacy in certain areas, even on private property. It is illegal to place hidden cameras in locations like restrooms, locker rooms, or changing rooms, where individuals expect privacy.114

A critical legal distinction is drawn between video and audio recording. While the law affords little protection from being visually recorded in public, it provides significantly stronger protections for the privacy of conversations. The federal Electronic Communications Privacy Act (ECPA) of 1986, along with various state wiretapping laws, imposes much stricter regulations on the interception and recording of audio communications.114
These laws create a legal split among the states regarding the consent required for audio recording:

listitem.para: One-Party Consent States: In the majority of states, it is legal to record a conversation as long as at least one party to that conversation consents.114 This means an individual can legally record their own conversations with others without informing the other parties.

All-Party Consent States: A minority of states, including California, Florida, and Illinois, require the consent of all parties to a conversation before it can be legally recorded.114 In these jurisdictions, recording a conversation without the knowledge and permission of everyone involved is a criminal offense.

section.para: This legal dichotomy demonstrates a higher societal and legal value placed on the privacy of oral communication compared to the privacy of one's physical presence in a public or semi-public space. While one's image can be captured without consent, one's words are afforded a greater degree of legal protection, marking a clear boundary in the landscape of non-consensual corporate surveillance.
